segments.text: Good morning, Rebecca.
We have our first submitter on Zoom.
We swap between Zoom and in the room. So we'll leave it to you.
We have 10 minutes with you this morning.
You are our first submitter on the Employment Leave Bill, so we're
pleased to hear from you. And that includes time for questions, which we'll leave
over to you to allow time. Over to you.
Thank you. Thank you for having me.
I'm Rebecca Graham, and I am the National Director for Parents of Visually
Impaired New Zealand. We're a national charity that provides
support and advocacy for parents and families of a blind,
low vision, deaf blind, or vision impaired child, young person.
Some of our members have adults that they provide care for.
The reason we submitted on this bill
is a lot of our families have one parent
who is the primary caregiving parent, and they work part-time,
and they're often juggling part-time work in and around caregiving
duties.
And it's a really important role. It's certainly much needed.
And we are concerned that this bill actually will
penalize our families a little bit.
I'm also aware that while we're submitting on behalf of our families, that
within the wider disability sector, this is often the case as well,
with people providing care for aging parents, and
spouses, and other family members.
But certainly our focus is on our parents and our
families.
With care responsibilities, of course, comes reduced
hours, reduced income, and reduced workforce participation.
One of the core issues with the bill as proposed is that it
ties entitlements and leave entitlements and sick leave
entitlements to the number of hours worked.
And this creates a type of
systemic disadvantage
for our families, and particularly for those who are working part-time hours
or who are already working fewer hours because of disability
or caregiving related needs. Under the proposed
hourly accrual, it means that fewer hours means
less annual leave. It means less sick leave.
And for those who are casual workers, it means
extremely limited leave.
And for casual workers in particular too, there's a loading that
gets replaced instead of leave, and it makes it much harder for them to take time
off. And that is quite concerning because for our
parents, particularly when you're providing care for someone, you do need
to be able to juggle things like appointments,
things like if the person you're caring for gets sick, being able to take leave to
care for them, being able to attend meetings.
I'm not sure if any of you have kids in education, but
most of us have what's called IEPs, which is an individual education
plan. That happens generally six monthly, and it just
about always is in work hours because of course that's when all the
other paid professionals can attend.
So our parents have to take time off work or rearrange their work schedule to be
able to attend.
And those changes, while small, actually do
have an impact for our families who are already facing disadvantage.
I'm also aware of the United Nations Convention on the
Rights of Persons with Disabilities, thinking about Articles 27 and
28, which is about employment. And the most recent
IMM, International Monitoring Mechanism,
report and the concluding observations for New Zealand, which talked about
employment and talked about strengthening employment and talked about
strengthening the fairness. And
that this bill, while it doesn't contradict that, it sort of
undermines it, and it certainly isn't upholding what is being recommended
there. The other issue, of course, is the
compounding hardship that this
bill allows to happen with about
21% of disabled children in New Zealand already live in
households experiencing material hardship.
And that is nearly twice as much when compared to non-disabled
children. And one of those reasons for that is that
the need for someone to be in that caregiving role, they're not being paid the
same. And this bill really does undermine some of
that really important leave that supports families and supports
whanau. It just creates it harder.
It really does. Reducing leave pay to the lowest
hourly rate creates a financial disincentive to take leave when sick.
We don't want our parents working when they're sick.
We want them to be able to rest, to look after themselves.
We certainly don't want their health worsening, and we don't want the poorer
long-term health outcomes that come when you're unable to take leave and look after
yourself.
So,
that very quick summary. We do wish to
see a retaining of the minimum of four weeks annual leave
regardless of hours worked. We do want to maintain
universal sick leave entitlements and not have them reduced by hours
worked.
We do want to retain access to leave for casual
workers who demonstrate regular work patterns.
And we also want to ensure that leave continues to
accrue during ACC caregiving and return to work periods.
We would like to retain current pay calculation methods so that workers are
not financially penalized for taking leave.
And
our concern is that there hasn't been a disability or
caregiving impact assessment before progressing this bill, and it would be
really good to have that more formally undertaken.And I
am going to stop there. So you've got, by my
calculation, three minutes for questions. If you have any questions.
If not, that is fine.
Thank you, Rebecca. We already have two ready for you, one from Carl Bates.
Yeah. Thanks very much, Chair. And look, really appreciate
some of the challenges that you've referred to and things that we can look at in
the bill process. The bit where I'm a little bit stuck is around the
sick leave question. So if you're a parent that
works half a day
every week on, say, a Monday morning,
your contention is that that parent should essentially be
able to have 10 weeks in a row sick leave, i.e.
be able to take every Monday morning as sick leave for
legitimate reasons maybe,
but essentially do that while their colleagues who work
full-time
wouldn't
be able to take 10 weeks sick leave
consecutively. And I just can't see where
you can argue that that's fair either on other
employees or on the employer who has to carry that cost.
And it makes me wonder if actually that's
disadvantageous to parents
like those that you're referring to who need part-time work,
because employers understand the higher cost of having part-time
work.
That is a hypothetical situation, and I'm not aware that that actually
occurs in reality.
I can give you examples of where that happens in reality.
So let's not go down that hypothetical
debate.
Yeah. I can see what you're saying, and that is a challenge for the select
committee to work through in terms of how do you uphold that.
And I think that's where having a proper
disability and caregiving type assessment would be really useful, because that
would then give the select committee that kind of economic
and expertise which would be able to provide
you with actually what is happening here, and what
could we do so that
your employees don't feel like someone's taking the mick.
But also, equivalently, our
parents are able to be supported and take the leave they need.
Thank you.
Because we do want our parents to be in paid work.
That is
a huge thing. We would like to support that.
Thank you. We've got one more question from Shanan Halbert.
Tena koe, Rebecca. Mihi ana ki a koe te outane.
Thanks very much for that submission.
I wondered if you could walk us through a real-life example of a
family that potentially you've worked with and what
these impacts will mean. But secondly, point to an example
where the impact assessment has been done in previous
work and why that would be beneficial in this instance.
Sure. So what we find
with our real-life families is when they don't have flexibility,
it's usually the mum,
and not always, but it's often the mum, the mum just quits.
And this particularly happens when the child ages out of school, because when the
child's in school, there's some support there.
Once the child ages out of school, there's nothing for that young
person to go into often, and then it's the mum, it's the mum who
quits work because they then have these extra demands that they
have to juggle, and they can't figure out how to do that if they don't have
flexible work. There is some flexible
work out there, but if you're thinking about a mum, they've got a
kid, they've had to work these part-time casualized hours,
they often don't have the luxury of having the types of professional work
that offers you the sort of flexibility that some of us enjoy.
And they're the families that I'm most concerned about because they're the ones
most at risk. They haven't had the luxury of being able to retrain or be in
professional jobs.
Thank you.
And that creates that type of poverty we're seeing, where someone has to
quit work. They're not having access to it.
Can I give you-
Rebecca, I'm so sorry to interrupt. We have reached the end of your time.
Oh.
We have a very, very full day.
Look at me blathering on.
No, look, it's lovely, and thank you so much for your participation in the process.
We really appreciate it.
We will need to move on now, so thank you, and have a great day.
Thanks.
And we have our next submitter all ready on Zoom.
We have got Mike Chapman, Chair of New Zealand Ethical Employers Incorporated.
Mike, you've probably heard this all before.
Ten minutes, includes time for questions, and we're over to you now.
Marina.
Our members employ over 20,000 people.
A lot of those workers are casual and seasonal workers, so our
submission focuses on that.
We've got four asks, and I'll go through them very quickly because hopefully our
written submission is clear enough.
First is we're suggesting that the committee
tighten or clarify the treatment of standard hours, additional hours,
and casual hours so that they reflect the
work patterns that
the people who are employed by our members actually work in.
Notional rosters also need, in our second
request, to really focus on how
our sector works. We're affected by the weather.
We're affected by whether fruit or vegetables
are ripe, and also market realities.
The market orders may dictate when things can be done.
The third request relates to the 12.5%
compensation.
We're a bit confused about this. So
non-permanent workers get 12.5% paid upfront.
Permanent workers don't. The thing about sick
leave is it's taken when it's needed.
So why would you pay a seasonal worker, a
casual worker, 12.5% at the start before they've
taken any sick leave, whereas your permanent worker is in
a different situation, where you assess that sick leave is taken out of the
12.5%?Yeah, the bill's doing a fantastic
thing. It's clarifying an area which has been a
massive problem in terms of just trying to do things
right, and so we really support that.
And therefore, our fourth request is we're really interested, when this
bill becomes law, that really good worked-up examples are
provided by MBIE, so that employers and workers
can make the bill work and make it work well. Thank you.
Thank you very much, Mike. That's incredibly helpful and constructive.
Do I have any questions from members?
If not, can I say, Mike, that is not a lack of interest.
That is a very comprehensive and constructive submission, so really appreciate it.
Thank you, and I can see our officials writing feverishly in the background.
So, we'll give you a few minutes back in your day.
Thank you very much. Thanks for your time.
Thanks so much, Mike.
Cheers, Mike.
Cheers. Bye.
Right.
Our next submitter, we have the New Zealand Airline Pilots Association, I think in
the room. Wonderful. Welcome, Imogen.
You've heard it all before. You've got 10 minutes.
We don't need to rush you to the table.
Thank you.
And so that 10 minutes includes time for questions, so if you can leave some time,
that'd be great.
Awesome. Thank you.
Obviously here to represent NZALPA as a part of our legal team.
So we look after 83 different employer groups, and we
advocate for fair employment conditions, safety, wellbeing, and
operational integrity within New Zealand's highly regulated
aviation sector.
So this submission today, obviously we'd like to discuss our concerns about the
interaction with aviation's unique operational realities, including
irregular hours, complex rostering, and fatigue management
obligations.
NZALPA would like to see sector-specific exemptions and continued
collaboration to ensure the bill promotes clarity and fairness
without undermining aviation safety, collective bargaining outcomes,
and operational stability for our regulators.
In terms of talking about each of our sort of big-ticket items separately, the
categories of working hours is one of our biggest issues that we are
currently looking at with the proposed bill.
The introduction of standard, additional, and casual hour
classifications represents significant departure from our
existing conditions. Aviation rostering is
highly complex and demand driven.
No working shift or rostering pattern is completely the same.
We've got weather constraints, we've got operational demands, flight
cancellations, the current constraints that we're facing with fuel prices and
the economy.
We've got a lot of demands to look at in that space.
One of our main concerns was the notional rostering pattern, and
if we were looking at how the labor inspectorate would apply the 13-week
period, there is no particular 13 weeks that would actually look
the same no matter what combination that you could actually draw from
the rostering patterns. So in terms of from an equity
perspective, we would like to sort of understand a little bit more about
whether or not there would be any exemptions in terms of application in that space
or how that would particularly work.
So that's one of the big items for us.
And in terms of talking about annual leave and sick leave accruals
all together at the same time, given the fact
that we do have varying different types of
shifts, when we're looking at standby callbacks, there's no particular
hours implemented. We would like to understand how
we would apply that in terms of a regular shift pattern.
It's
very different for every different employee.
We also, when we're looking at the sick leave pro-proration, a lot of our
employees are actually part-time, and we have sometimes three to five different
categorizations of part-time employees across our different collective
agreements. So when we're looking at how the part-time piece
would work, we would see, in terms of the fatigue management piece, a lot
of our part-time employees being significantly impacted within the aviation
side and within other public services.
Obviously, heightened sick leave is a common thing
every day. With the bereavement leave and the family
violence leave part day entitlements, at the moment it's
near impossible for us to ensure our employees to have part day shifts given
the fact that they are shifts that can't be disrupted 9 times out of
10. So it'd be very hard for us to see part day entitlements to be
enacted.
The otherwise working day test, if the statutory 50%
test overrides bargain provisions, members risk indirect
disadvantage, loss of certainty, increased disputes, and compliance
uncertainty.
Leave payments,
what we're sort of looking at as another big-ticket item is the reconciliation
piece. Given that
we have obviously different rostering constraints, if
we were to agree to some form of notional roster, we would have
to see, obviously, the next pay period or agreements between the parties
of the collective agreements to agree to a reconciliation
period, which would actually be really hard for us.
And we've sort of projected that the wage arrears claims would go through the roof
in our sector, given if we can't manage how these reconciliation
periods are, and we would rely on employees coming forward
to us consistently to point out that their leave accruals haven't
been administered correctly.
The proposed leave compensation payment of 12.5%
may be appropriate for genuine casual employment but represents a
concerning shift for permanent employees.
Under the bill, additional hours worked beyond standard hours would no longer
enhance leave entitlements. In aviation, variable and
extended hours are inherent to permanent employment.
The approach effectively recharacterizes ordinary work as quasi-casual,
erodes leave value over time, and risks undermining fatigue and
safety frameworks.When we talk about the
remediation of historical non-compliance, given that the
vast majority of our employer groups are shaped by the
current Holidays Leave Act provisions, and given the complex
rostering,
current sort of remediation in terms of rest and meal breaks,
arrears claims, and things like that, we do rely on the calculations that are
going through the Holidays Act. And for us to sort of move
away from the current precedents in place would create a huge
ripple effect through all of our collective agreements with how rostering works
and how leave will be calculated.
To sort of conclude, our sort of
big-ticket items are we really would like to see greater flexibility in terms of
the classification of working hours,
to recommend to become aligned with the operational realities of
aviation work, and that no day is the same.
Support is given to obviously cashing up 25% of annual leave
entitlements, as long as it obviously remains in that voluntary space.
Standard leave entitlements and accrual mechanisms, we'd like to see some
form of exemption in that space, given that we can't
see there being any certainty around the sick leave and annual leave accruals from
an equity perspective, given that all of the roster periods derived
are completely different. And we would have to negotiate on a
notional roster that we could have set in place, but the
realized hours would automatically create a reconciliation
every single time. So we'd like to obviously stick with the status
quo.
A clear and adaptable definition of part-day leave should be included in the
legislation, allowing mutual agreement on its application within operational
context. The statutory otherwise working day test should
operate as a default mechanism to avoid overriding collectively
bargained provisions, with contractual provisions prevailing if they're more
preferable. Leave payment calculations should reflect actual
earnings, including variable components, to preserve fairness and economic
protection for employees with fluctuating income patterns.
A hybrid model for alternative holiday entitlements is recommended,
allowing hourly accruals to be into full days' worth of
leave, with a minimum entitlement floor for employees working on public
holidays. And clearly for something both employees and employers
would like, flexibility in terms of pay statements.
We
have sort of anticipated a huge reconciliation period that would
happen.
And obviously, sector-specific accommodations are necessary for the
remediation of historical non-compliance.
At the end of the day, we do just have considerable concerns around the
rostering constraints and what that's going to mean for accruals and
correct application.
Does anyone have any questions?
Yes.
Thank you.
So we have three questions lined up-
... and not a lot of time, so can I ask members to be brief with the kind of
question so that we can get a brief answer? Thank you.
Carl first.
Thanks, Chair. In section 8, you talk about the
cashing up.
Yes.
And saying that's a good thing. And then in section 10, you talk
about the need for genuine rest and recreation.
Yes.
And those two concepts just challenge each other.
So I was interested in your thought.
Yes. I think from an employee engagement perspective,
we're happy to obviously allow for...
We don't promote it within aviation to cash up your annual leave.
We're more saying we support the cashing-up request if it remains
autonomous in terms of you can select what you cash up, and there's mutual
agreement in that space. But we do, our position is to promote people to
take annual leave and use it for rest and recreation.
That's why one of our main oppositions was only being able to
take obviously one period of 14-day leave outside
of mutual agreement within the anniversary period.
So yeah, we do obviously support completely people
taking leave.
Thanks.
Yeah.
Parmjeet Parmar.
Yeah, thank you. So about the part-day leave-
Yes
... you have expressed your concerns about that.
So what happens in a situation when somebody's on a shift and falls sick?
So in that situation, the person-
Yeah
... will have to go, and so you already might be managing that, isn't it, in a way?
Yeah. So it is a current obviously reality that we face, and that's why sometimes
we have flight cancellations and things like that, or well, it happens all the
time. So if someone is obviously sick or fatigued, they would have to remove
themselves from their shift, and then they have to either call in someone from
standby or callback sort of component, or there's someone ready to go.
In that instance, obviously, that is a part-day mechanism that
would potentially come into place, but obviously, it's an actual
full day worth of leave taken dependent on the collective agreement.
So there are some benefits, I do acknowledge in that sense.
But in terms of the application, calling up in the morning, "I've got to take my
child to an appointment. Can I have a half day?" It's a full
shift realized if that-
Yeah
... answers that question. Yeah.
Thank you. And then a final question from Shannon Halbert.
Thanks so much. And hey,
the sector's done a lot of work around the fatigue management
framework-
Mm-hmm
... over a few years. And in hearing your summary
today, that this really is counter
to what the sector's been trying to achieve in that space.
Is that your understanding, and if so, why?
I think in terms of the accrual rates, like if we're looking at sick leave
and rostering constraints in itself, in terms of the fatigue management
perspective piece, I would say the sick leave proration would be
our main concern in terms of fatigue management.
And that's purely because obviously, if you're reducing someone's leave
entitlements, they're more likely to go to work feeling unwell.
Mm.
And given that aviation professionals quite often get unwell, given
the sector that they work in and the hours that they work, and the fatigue they
do face every day, reducing their entitlement,
while we don't have the statistics, but I could imagine that it would increase
sick leave through the roof and risk to the public sector, given people might
choose to operate in an unsafe environment.
Thank you.
Yeah.
Thank you very much.
Thank you very much.
Really appreciate your submission, being part of the process.
Thank you.
Nice to have someone in person sometimes.
I know. Thank you.
Awesome. All right. We move on now to Horticulture New Zealand.
Sarah Cameron and Dean Smith, welcome.You're online.
We have 10 minutes with you. That includes time for questions, and I'll leave
you to manage that. And it's over to you now.
So good morning, everybody. There are two issues of
concern for us noted in our submission, which is the LCP
payment and the otherwise working day test.
And we mirror Mike Chapman's comments around the
LCP payment.
So fully aware we've only got a short time for questions, so I'll just quickly hand
over to Dean, who will have some
comments to make on both of those.
Kia ora, everyone. Thanks, Sarah.
Look, I suppose from a Summerfruit NZ perspective, we obviously represent
growers of cherries, peaches, plums, apricots, nectarines in New Zealand.
Our primary concern is especially for the cherry industry, and I think we need to
consider the dynamics of that industry.
So,
it's an industry which has a very compressed season, so starts around
December,
finishes in early February. So it's normally around a five- to six-week sprint.
So it's an industry that relies very, very heavily on casual labor,
and
I guess the context around that is the material impact that the proposed
changes would have on the profitability of that industry by
baking in sick leave entitlements into the LCP.
So for context, around 40% of total operating expenditure
for an average cherry orchard is casual
labor. So, by baking in an additional 4.5% sick leave
on 40% of your
opex, that's effectively a 2% increase in total expense.
And when you're operating with gross margins in the vicinity of
10% in an average year,
this year won't be anything like that.
Obviously, effectively that's
2% of your profit gone by baking this in.
And I think the other thing, we acknowledge the importance of sick
leave, but I guess by baking it in from day one and
making it effectively an additional fixed cost, that's a major
concern.
I guess the other aspect of this is around the otherwise
working day aspect.
The cherry industry is also unique in that it has an unavoidable
timeframe, which coincides with a significant number of public
holidays. So effectively, you're looking at around
8% to 9% of working days are public holidays, which are obviously
unavoidable.
And we don't feel that that's equitable.
It's not currently, and the bill doesn't resolve it.
In the submission, you'll see that we believe a more equitable approach
would be to deal with that by
adding an additional margin on top of
the casual wage. Sarah, you might want to
elaborate on what's proposed.
Yeah. So we're proposing a 4% across the
board payment. And I do have a point of clarification around that.
In the submission that was consulted on throughout the industry, that
4% is for an eight-week period. So for casual workers that are
employed less than eight weeks, that 4% would apply to
account for those
condensed public holidays over the Christmas period for the cherry industry,
not for casual workers, no matter how long they are employed.
So we've based the calculation on the
number of public holidays there are per year versus the 260
working days, which is noted in the Cabinet paper.
And I guess, yeah, we invite questions now.
Wonderful. Thank you. I do have a question myself.
I understand the difference
with the casual nature, or the
seasonal nature of your casual employment.
When it comes to your perspective, I
suppose, on the changes,
I just want to understand, I think, what sort of
preparation there is for any change that
ever could happen. Any change in this particular case, and
I really understand everything that you're coming through with.
In terms of the 2% increase,
we've seen RSE changes over time, how the sector's
dealt with that and cost implications and sort of the preparation for
things like this that might happen.
Yeah, look, I guess we appreciate that inflation happens, and that you have
to have a business model that can absorb inflation.
I guess this just comes on top of everything else, and the materiality of it for
this part of the industry is probably the thing that's the most alarming.
As I say,
we have a disproportional reliance on casual labor, and it's
unavoidable, and the casual people that we are calling on
are probably perhaps not the people that were perceived to be
who the changes were
to consider, in that we are talking about backpackers, we're talking
about students.
These are the people that we rely on to harvest cherries at this peak time.
And so for context, you might have
an operator that might have four or five permanent staff, but their
workforce might swell to 50 or 60 backpackers and students
that they rely on at that time.
So we can't get away from the impact of it,
and I guess it's the materiality of the impact on profitability which
we are really wanting to highlight, I suppose.
If I said to you that,
for instance, if your gross margin's 10% and 2%'s taken away, that's a
20% reduction in your profit.
If your boss rocked up to you and said, "Hey, by the way, your salary's going to
increase by 20%," I don't think you'd consider that immaterial.
Yeah. And thank you. That's-
And if I could just-- Sorry. If I could-
Yeah, no, go
... yeah, a comment around, you raised the RSE scheme, and we are
in a situation now with that scheme where it has
become-Probably not economically viable
for employers to continue using it, and we're seeing big drop-offs
in the numbers of employers utilizing the scheme because of the
financial burden attached to it around the pastoral care.
So,
employers are really facing it out there, and they're having to
make choices
which impact their bottom line.
Thank you. That's incredibly helpful context to especially the earlier part of the
question.
It doesn't appear that we have any further questions, but can I please say that
it's not a lack of interest. You have put forward a very, very helpful
and comprehensive submission, and I know our officials will really appreciate it.
Awesome.
So thank you for that.
Thanks.
Thank you.
Thanks for the opportunity.
Thank you for being part of the process. Yeah, thank you very much.
All right, bye.
Bye.
All right. We have Access Community Health Limited, and
we are running a little early now, so my hope is that they are
available. Wonderful. We have got Warwick Jackson
joining us
online.
Good morning.
Good morning, Warwick. We have 10 minutes with you this morning, and that includes
time for questions, so we'll leave you to manage that, and hand over to you
now.
Yeah. Tena koe chair, members of the committee.
Thank you for the opportunity to represent Access Community Health regarding the
Employment Leave Bill. I intend to only speak for five minutes and leave time for
questions.
My name is Warwick Jackson. I am the Executive Director, Strategy and Finance
for Access.
Access Community Health is a New Zealand-based home and community
organization. We help tens of thousands of people every week live their
lives at home every day. To do this, we have
3,500 amazing staff. This includes nurses,
people who work in the office, but also 3,000 support workers.
So support workers are quite a unique
group. They mostly work part-time, maybe 20 to 30
hours a week on average. Their work often changes because the people we
support have changing needs. They move in and out of our service.
So rosters change weekly, week on week, daily.
Hours change, frequency changes.
It's very
difficult to manage. With all that complexity and volume, all the
edge cases
come up commonly for our sort of business.
So any sort of scenario that you think, "Oh, that might be a bit unlikely," we
probably get it. So that's why this bill is so important to
us.
So firstly, I say we support the bill, and it's moved from a weeks-based
system to an hours accrual system.
However, I want to focus on one particular part of our
submission that the bill, as written, has the
potential to create a perverse incentive for
employees to at times not take their sick
leave and to not agree to standard hours.
A sick health worker should not be working, as they put the health of
themselves and the vulnerable people we care for at risk.
The most important change we are looking for is to either
remove the LCP payment system altogether for non-casual staff
or allow it to be optional so leave can be accrued
on all hours worked.
As an example to illustrate this, a support worker often
begins with very few hours, maybe five a week, and they build up their hours
if they want to. And let's say they build up those hours to 40 hours
per week. So in the extreme, that person has got five
standard hours and 35 additional hours.
So that's 35 hours that they're getting paid LCP for, the
12.5%. When the employer proposes
converting those hours from additional hours to standard hours to better
affect the worker's ongoing schedule, the employee faces a
rational economic choice to refuse this.
Converting would mean losing the immediate LCP payment
because they're getting more. They're getting it in their pocket.
They don't have to wait for it. Yes, they're exchanging it for not
accruing leave, but in terms of
that choice for that week, you're effectively decreasing your pay.
So where the worker is encouraged to not accept standard hours and the employer
has a need for hours, this puts the employer in a precarious
